peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong support for democratic norms and high political trust align with endorsing loser consent, though low agreeableness may reduce full commitment.
High system justification and trust in institutions, combined with high agreeableness and conventionalism, support accepting electoral outcomes as legitimate.
Moderate support arises from very high agreeableness and fairness values, but weak external political efficacy and a competitive worldview create ambivalence.
High political trust (84.84) and civic nationalism (90.06) support a firm commitment to democratic norms, though a moderate authoritarian submission (28.81) tempers the score slightly.

Strong civic nationalism (75.78) and high agreeableness (95.64) support fair-play norms, while very low system justification (5.37) and low RWA (19.7) reduce deference to winners, yielding clear but not maximal endorsement.

Very high agreeableness and high trust in institutions, alongside a strong commitment to fairness and integrity, produce clear support for the norm of loser consent, though moderate system justification tempers a more maximal stance.

Strong system justification (74.62) and high authoritarianism (79.42) support procedural legitimacy, yet very low social dominance orientation (0.97) and low ethnic nationalism (0.12) prevent maximal endorsement.

High socio-cultural liberalism (93.18) and strong fairness/equality moral foundation (73.59) support democratic norms, but low conscientiousness and moderate political trust (69.83) prevent maximal endorsement.
